text:
University College Hospital

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr Susan
University College Hospital
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, London WC1N 3BG

Dear Dr Susan,

William Harris, DOB: 15-02-1965, Hospital No: H563829 NHS No. 9273645810
10 Meadow Park, London, EC1V 9BT

Summary/ Diagnoses: Epilepsy following remote head injury; persistent cognitive difficulties; osteoporosis; history of alcohol dependence with prior alcoholic hepatitis; currently residing in supported accommodation; reports abstinence from alcohol; current smoker.

Medication: Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily.
Carbamazepine (Tegretol Retard) 400 mg twice daily.
Topiramate 75 mg in the morning and 100 mg at night.
Vitamin B compound strong, Thiamine, Calcium/vitamin D (Calcichew), Strontium sachets, Atorvastatin, Lansoprazole, Bisoprolol, Ramipril, Carbocisteine, Aqueous cream.

Dietary therapy: The patient is following a ketogenic/modified low-carbohydrate regimen overseen by an external dietetics service. He provided a written plan noting a 3:1 fat-to-carbohydrate+protein target on main meals, restricted total daily carbohydrate to approximately 20–30 g, emphasis on unsaturated fats (olive oil, avocado, nuts), measured portions, and daily ketone monitoring (urine strips) with typical readings reported as “moderate to high.” He maintains a meal log reviewed fortnightly by that service. No changes were made by us today; we recorded the details in the hospital notes.

History and interval events: I saw him in clinic today without a support worker; he attended independently. He reports improved energy in the mornings since starting the dietary programme three months ago and fewer post-lunch dips. Appetite stable; weight down by approximately 2 kg over this period, which he attributes to diet structure. No intercurrent infections. He is walking with a single-point stick outdoors and uses no aid indoors; no recent falls. Cognitive complaints unchanged (word-finding and slowed processing); he uses reminder cards for appointments and medication.

Seizures: He keeps a simple calendar record and says there have been no episodes brought to attention by carers or bystanders, nor any events he has recognised as seizures, since early summer. He recalls no loss of awareness, convulsions, or nocturnal tongue-biting in this interval. He will continue to log any events and will notify us should that change.

Examination: Blood pressure 128/76, pulse 66 regular. Weight 71 kg (down from 73 kg at last review). Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations unremarkable. Neurologically, mild right-sided pyramidal pattern with increased tone at the ankle, power otherwise 5/5, reflexes brisk on the right, plantar flexor bilaterally. Gait slow but safe with good foot clearance in clinic corridor. No pressure areas on feet.

Plan:
- Continue current anti-seizure medication without change.
- Record of ketogenic/modified diet filed; we will copy details to primary care for information. No adjustments from our side; continues under external dietary supervision.
- Routine bloods (FBC, U&E, LFTs, bone profile, vitamin D) via GP within the next month, given dietary therapy and ongoing medications.
- Continue seizure diary and carer observations; he has been advised to seek review if any events occur or if diet leads to symptomatic hypoglycaemia, dehydration, or gastrointestinal issues.
- Follow-up in neurology clinic in 6 months, sooner if concerns arise.

Yours sincerely,

entities.text: there have been no episodes brought to attention by carers or bystanders, nor any events he has recognised as seizures, since early summer

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly discusses seizures. It states that he has kept a calendar and reports no episodes recognized as seizures since early summer. Clinic date is 02 October 2025. 'Early summer' typically implies around June 2025. From early June to early October is approximately 4 months; even if 'early summer' is late May, that is about 4 to 4.5 months. The criterion for 'seizure free' requires at least 6 months, which is not met based on the provided timeframe. Therefore, the appropriate normalized label is 'seizure free for multiple months.' No other numeric seizure frequency is provided, and there are no conflicting frequencies to reconcile.
seizure_frequency_number: there have been no episodes brought to attention by carers or bystanders, nor any events he has recognised as seizures, since early summer
